claim: The UK 4 Day Week Global pilot (2022, 61 companies, ~3,000 employees) found 92% of companies continued the 4-day week afterward, and only 4% definitively ruled it out.
In the UK 2022 pilot, company revenue rose 35% on average compared with the same period from previous years, and grew 1.4% on average during the six-month trial itself.
In the UK 2022 pilot, employee turnover dropped 57% and absenteeism (sick days) fell 65% across participating companies.
Iceland conducted two large-scale public-sector 4-day workweek pilots between 2015 and 2019 involving approximately 2,500 workers, reducing weekly hours from 40 to 35-36 with no pay cut.
After Iceland's trials, trade unions negotiated reduced hours across the workforce; by 2019 approximately 86-90% of Iceland's workforce had shifted to shorter schedules (35-36 hours per week).
Iceland's economy grew 5% in 2023 (second highest among wealthy European economies) and GDP grew a further 1.3% in 2025, with unemployment at 3.4-3.6% in 2023 — roughly half the European average.
In Iceland's trials, 62% of workers on reduced hours reported being more satisfied with their schedules and 97% believed shorter hours made it easier to balance work with personal life.

Buffer (social media SaaS) implemented a 4-day workweek in May 2020 and made it permanent; 91% of employees reported being happier and more productive, 99.1% reported less stress, and only 7% felt less productive.
Buffer found 84% of employees could complete their work within four days, while 40.4% felt they could not finish everything; 61.6% used the fifth day as an overflow day at least some of the time.

Bolt (fintech, $14B valuation) piloted a 4-day workweek in late 2021: 84% of employees reported improved work-life balance, 84% said they were more productive, and the company saw a 200% increase in job applicants.
Bolt quietly eliminated its 4-day workweek after CEO Ryan Breslow returned and cited 'significant gaps in execution'; the policy was removed despite initial positive results.
Kickstarter piloted a 4-day, 32-hour workweek in April 2022 under the '100-80-100' model; goal achievement jumped from 62% to 95% and employee retention rose from 82% to 98%.
Kickstarter's employee engagement surged 50% and work-life balance satisfaction increased from 48% to 81% after adopting the 4-day workweek.

Kickstarter CEO Everette Taylor stated the 4-day model is 'not all good' — it attracts some applicants who just want the perk, and 'the level of intensity, intention and velocity that you have to bring in everything that you do is extreme.'
In 2025, after a 42-day strike, the Kickstarter United union secured contractual protections for the 4-day workweek, preventing management from unilaterally changing the schedule.

Weglot (SaaS translation platform) tested a 32-hour 4-day workweek in Q4 2023-Q1 2024; 100% of the team was satisfied, well-being survey scores rose from 82% to 92%, and 100% retention was maintained during probation for three consecutive years.

SOAX (SaaS proxy platform) piloted a 4-day workweek under the 100-80-100 model; 80% of employees reported lower stress, 98% wanted to continue permanently, and 44% reported getting even more work done.

Loka (tech consultancy) implemented every-other-Friday off starting January 2022; through 2024 they nearly tripled project launches per year, achieved 95% employee retention, and gained 7,000-10,000 more job applicants annually.
Microsoft Japan trialed a 4-day workweek with 2,300 employees and reported a 40% productivity boost; employees also took 25% less time off during the trial.
A global 18-month study by 4 Day Week Global covering the US, Canada, UK, and Ireland found participating employers reported a 15% increase in revenue over the course of the trial.
A South Africa 4-day workweek pilot found company revenue grew by an average of 10.5% among participating firms.

YLaw (Vancouver law firm) reported revenues increased 30% within three months of implementing a 4-day workweek pilot; the firm tripled in size in two years and did not lose a single person to a competitor.

There are two primary 4-day workweek models: the compressed model (same 40 hours over 4 days, typically 10-hour shifts) and the reduced-hours model (32 hours, 80% time, full pay, known as the '100-80-100' model).
Belgium introduced legislation in 2022 allowing employees to compress their standard workweek into four days, but only about 1% of workers chose this option.

Juliet Schor's multi-year research across 400+ companies and roughly 13,000 employees found that approximately 90% of organizations retained the 4-day schedule after trialing it, with a roughly 13% average productivity increase.

A Boston College cross-national trial (211+ businesses, 7,200+ employees) found over 95% of employees preferred the 4-day week after the trial; the median pay raise required to return to a 5-day week was 26-50%.

The UK's second 4-day week trial (November 2024 - April 2025, 17 companies, ~1,000 employees) was declared '100% success' with every company opting to continue; 12 adopted permanent 4-day weeks and 5 moved to a nine-day fortnight.

In the UK's second trial (2024-2025), 54% of employees reported increased 'working smart' (greater focus and efficiency), 62% experienced burnout less frequently, and 41% reported improvements in mental health.
The 4 Day Week Foundation has accredited over 235 UK companies that permanently adopted a 4-day week without salary cuts as of 2025, representing over 6,000 workers.
